paragraphs: Article 1. This Law establishes general national standards for the responsible governance of artificial intelligence (AI) systems in Brazil, with the objective of protecting fundamental rights, stimulating responsible innovation and competitiveness and guaranteeing the implementation of safe and reliable systems, for the benefit of the human person, the democratic regime and social, scientific, technological and economic development.
§ 1. This Law does not apply to the AI system:
I – used by a natural person for exclusively private and non-economic purposes;
II – developed and used solely and exclusively for national defense purposes;

III – used in investigation, research, testing, and development of AI systems, applications, or models before they are placed on the market or put into service, provided that the applicable legislation is observed for those activities, especially Law No. 8,078 of 11 September 1990 (Consumer Protection Code), Law No. 13,709 of 14 August 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), Law No. 6,938 of 31 August 1981 (National Environmental Policy), and Law No. 9,610 of 19 February 1998 (Copyright Law), and provided that testing under real-world conditions complies with this Law;
IV – used in services that are limited to providing infrastructure for storing and transporting data used in AI systems.

§ 2. In order to promote national technological development, the National System for AI Regulation and Governance (SIA) shall regulate simplified regimes, involving relaxation of regulatory obligations provided for in this Law, in the following cases:
I – open and free standards and formats, with the exception of those considered high risk;

II – encouraging innovation and scientific and technological research in the productive environment, with a view to technological training, the achievement of technological autonomy and the development of the country's national and regional production system;
III – projects of public interest, those that meet the priorities of industrial and science, technology and innovation policies and those that aim to solve Brazilian problems.
Article 2. The development, implementation and use of AI systems in Brazil are based on:
I – centrality of the human person;
II – respect and promotion of human rights and democratic values;
III – free development of personality and freedom of expression;
IV – protection of the environment and ecologically balanced development;
V – equality, non-discrimination, plurality and diversity;
VI – social rights, especially the appreciation of human work;
VII – socioeconomic, scientific and technological development and innovation;
VIII – consumer protection, free enterprise and free competition;
IX – privacy, protection of personal data and informational self-determination;

X – promotion of research and development to foster social development, reduce inequalities, and encourage innovation in productive sectors and public administration, as well as public-private partnerships;
XI – access to information and data dissemination, in an open, structured and secure manner;
XII – protection of cultural rights and promotion of artistic and historical assets;
XIII – education and awareness about AI systems to promote full development and the exercise of citizenship;

XIV – protection and promotion of the rights of vulnerable groups, especially the elderly, people with disabilities and, with full protection and aiming for the best interests, children and adolescents, recognizing their heightened vulnerability;

XV – integrity of information through the protection and promotion of reliability, precision and consistency of information to strengthen freedom of expression, access to information and other fundamental rights;
XVI – strengthening the democratic process and political pluralism;
XVII – protection of copyright and related rights, intellectual property rights and commercial and industrial secrets;
XVIII – guarantee of information security and cybersecurity;
XIX – Brazilian insertion, integration and competitiveness in the international market;
XX – international cooperation for the development and compliance with technical standards and national and international obligation regimes.
Article 3. The development, implementation and use of AI systems shall observe good faith and the following principles:
I – inclusive growth, sustainable development and well-being, including the protection of work and workers;
II – self-determination and freedom of decision and choice;
III – effective and adequate human supervision and determination in the AI life cycle, considering the degree of risk involved;
IV – prohibition of unlawful or abusive discrimination;
V – justice, equity and inclusion;
VI – transparency and explainability, observing commercial and industrial secrets, considering the participation of each agent in the AI value chain;
VII – due diligence and auditability throughout the entire life cycle of the AI system, according to the risk involved and the state of the art of technological development;
VIII – reliability and robustness of the AI system;
IX – protection of fundamental rights and guarantees, including due legal process, contestability and adversarial proceedings;
X – accountability, responsibility and full compensation for damages;
XI – prevention, precaution and mitigation of risks and damages;
XII – non-maleficence and proportionality between the methods used and the determined and legitimate purposes of the AI system;
XIII – ethical and responsible development and use of AI;
XIV – transparent, participatory governance oriented towards the protection of individual, social, collective and economic fundamental rights;
XV – promoting interoperability of AI systems to enable broader access and collaborative innovation;
XVI – possibility and condition for the use of systems and technologies safely and autonomously by people with disabilities, guaranteeing full accessibility to information and communication;
XVII – full protection of children and adolescents.
Article 4. For the purposes of this Law, the following definitions are adopted:

I – artificial intelligence (AI) system: machine-based system that, with different degrees of autonomy and for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from a set of data or information it receives, how to generate results, in particular prediction, content, recommendation or decision that may influence the virtual, physical or real environment;

II – life cycle: series of phases, from conception, planning, development, training, retraining, testing, validation, implementation and monitoring, to eventual modifications and adaptations of an AI system, whose discontinuity may occur in any of the aforementioned stages;

III – general-purpose artificial intelligence system (SIAPG): an AI system based on an AI model trained on large-scale datasets, capable of performing a wide range of distinct tasks and serving different purposes, including tasks for which it was not specifically developed and trained, and capable of being integrated into different systems or applications;

IV – generative artificial intelligence (generative AI): AI model specifically intended to generate or significantly modify, with different degrees of autonomy, text, images, audio, video or software code;

V – developer: a public- or private-sector natural or legal person that develops an AI system, directly or on commission, with a view to placing it on the market or applying it to a service that it provides, under its own name or brand, for remuneration or free of charge;
VI – distributor: a public- or private-sector natural or legal person that makes an AI system available and distributes it for use by third parties, for remuneration or free of charge;

VII – deployer: a public- or private-sector natural or legal person that employs or uses an AI system on its own behalf or for its own benefit, including by configuring or maintaining the system or supporting the provision of data for its operation and monitoring;
VIII – artificial intelligence agents: developers, distributors and deployers who work in the value chain and internal governance of AI systems, under the terms defined by regulation;
IX – competent authority: federal public administration entity, endowed with technical and decision-making autonomy, which shall coordinate the National System for AI Regulation and Governance (SIA);

X – National System for AI Regulation and Governance (SIA): a regulatory ecosystem coordinated by the competent authority whose primary purpose is to promote and ensure cooperation and harmonization among the other sectoral authorities and regulatory bodies, without a relationship of hierarchical subordination among them, and with other national systems, for the full implementation and enforcement of this Law throughout the national territory, with legal certainty;

XI – unlawful or abusive discrimination: any distinction, exclusion, restriction, or preference, in any area of public or private life, whose purpose or effect is unlawfully or abusively to nullify or restrict the recognition, enjoyment, or exercise, on equal terms, of one or more rights or freedoms provided for in the legal order, on the basis of personal characteristics;

XII – unlawful or abusive indirect discrimination: discrimination that occurs when an apparently neutral rule, practice, or criterion is capable of disadvantaging an affected person or group, or placing them at a disadvantage, where that rule, practice, or criterion is unlawful or abusive;

XIII – text and data mining: process of extracting and analyzing large amounts of data, with a high degree of automation, carried out directly on primary data, or indirectly through another tool, from which patterns and correlations are extracted that shall generate relevant information for the research, development or use of AI systems;
XIV – affected person or group: natural person or group of people who are directly or indirectly impacted by an AI system;

XV – preliminary assessment: simplified self-assessment process, prior to the use or placing on the market of one or more AI systems, to classify their level of risk, with the aim of determining compliance with the obligations defined in this Law;

XVI – algorithmic impact assessment: analysis of the impact on fundamental rights, presenting preventive, mitigating and reversing measures for negative impacts, as well as measures to enhance the positive impacts of an AI system;

XVII – vulnerability: state of aggravated asymmetry of information or power that affects individuals or groups due, among others, to their cognitive, social, ethnic, economic and age conditions, such as children and adolescents, the elderly and people with disabilities;

XVIII – experimental regulatory environment (regulatory sandbox): set of special conditions established to develop, train, validate and test, for a limited time, an innovative AI system, as well as innovative business models and public policies and experimental techniques and technologies involving AI, by complying with previously established criteria and limits and through a facilitated procedure;
XIX – state of the art of technological development: reasonable and available technical means, based on scientific and technological evidence and consolidated good practices;
XX – relevant legal effects: negative modifying, impeding or extinguishing legal consequences that affect fundamental rights and freedoms;
XXI – synthetic content: information, such as images, videos, audio and text, that has been significantly modified or generated by AI systems;

XXII – information integrity: result of an information ecosystem that enables and makes reliable, diverse and accurate information and knowledge available in a timely manner to promote freedom of expression;
XXIII – biometric identification: method that involves the recognition of human physical, physiological and behavioral characteristics, with the purpose of identifying an individual;

XXIV – biometric authentication: process of verifying or confirming an individual's identity, with the aim of uniquely identifying them, through the comparison of their biometric characteristics obtained from a previously stored model;
XXV – introduction or release into circulation on the market: initial availability or introduction to users of the AI system, whether paid or free of charge;

XXVI – sectoral authorities: bodies and entities of the federal Executive Branch responsible for regulating specific sectors of economic and governmental activity, according to their legal competence;
XXVII – autonomous weapons systems (SAA): systems that, once activated, can select and attack targets without additional human intervention;
XXVIII – application programming interface (API): set of protocols and functions that allow different systems to interact with each other;
XXIX – context of use: specific use for which the AI system or application is intended, including the system to be used, the specific context and purpose and its conditions of use;
XXX – systemic risk: potential negative adverse effects arising from a general-purpose and generative AI system with a significant impact on individual and social fundamental rights.
Article 5. The person or group affected by an AI system, regardless of their level of risk, has the following rights, to be exercised in the manner and under the conditions described in this Chapter:

I – right to information regarding their interactions with AI systems, in an accessible, free and easy-to-understand manner, including the automated nature of the interaction, except in cases where these are AI systems dedicated solely and exclusively to cybersecurity and cyberdefense, as per regulation;

II – right to privacy and protection of personal data, in particular the rights of data subjects under Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), and the relevant legislation;
III – right to freedom from unlawful or abusive discrimination and to the correction of direct or indirect unlawful or abusive discriminatory bias.
§ 1. The information referred to in item I of the chapeau of this article shall be provided using easily recognizable uniform icons or symbols, without prejudice to other formats.

§ 2. AI systems aimed at vulnerable groups shall, at all stages of their life cycle, be transparent and adopt simple, clear language appropriate to age and cognitive capacity, and be implemented considering the best interests of these groups.
Article 6. The person or group affected by a high-risk AI system has the following rights:
I – right to an explanation about the decision, recommendation or forecast made by the system;
II – right to contest and request a review of AI system decisions, recommendations or predictions;
III – right to human review of decisions, taking into account the context, risk and state of the art of technological development.

§ 1. The explanation referred to in item I of the chapeau of this article, respecting commercial and industrial secrets, shall include sufficient, adequate and intelligible information, in accordance with the regulations.

§ 2. The rights provided for in this Section shall be implemented considering the state of the art of technological development, and the agent of the high-risk AI system shall always implement effective and proportionate measures.

Article 7. The right to explanation provided for in this Section shall be provided through a free process, in simple, accessible and appropriate language that makes it easier for the person to understand the result of the decision or prediction in question, within a reasonable period of time, depending on the complexity of the AI system and the number of agents involved.

Sole paragraph. The competent authority shall regulate deadlines and procedures for exercising the right to explanation, including a simplified procedure, considering, among others, the following criteria:
I – the complexity of AI systems;
II – the size of the agent, especially in the case of micro and small companies and startups.

paragraphs: Article 8. Human oversight of high-risk AI systems shall seek to prevent or minimize risks to the rights and freedoms of affected persons or groups that may arise from normal use or reasonably foreseeable misuse, enabling the persons responsible for human oversight, in accordance with regulations, to understand, interpret, decide, and intervene in AI systems and to prioritize the management of risks and irreversible impacts.

Sole paragraph. Human supervision shall not be required if its implementation is demonstrably impossible or involves disproportionate effort, in which cases the high-risk AI system agent shall implement effective alternative measures.

Article 8. Human oversight of high-risk AI systems shall seek to prevent or minimize risks to the rights and freedoms of affected persons or groups that may arise from normal use or reasonably foreseeable misuse, enabling the persons responsible for human oversight, in accordance with regulations, to understand, interpret, decide, and intervene in AI systems and to prioritize the management of risks and irreversible impacts.

Sole paragraph. Human supervision shall not be required if its implementation is demonstrably impossible or involves disproportionate effort, in which cases the high-risk AI system agent shall implement effective alternative measures.

paragraphs: Article 9. High-risk AI agents shall inform, in a sufficient, objective, clear and accessible way, the procedures necessary to exercise the rights described in this Chapter.
units.fullText: Article 9. High-risk AI agents shall inform, in a sufficient, objective, clear and accessible way, the procedures necessary to exercise the rights described in this Chapter.

paragraphs: Article 10. The competent authority shall establish, where appropriate and always in formal institutional cooperation with the SIA sectoral authorities, general guidelines on the form and conditions for exercising rights before each of the AI system agents.
units.fullText: Article 10. The competent authority shall establish, where appropriate and always in formal institutional cooperation with the SIA sectoral authorities, general guidelines on the form and conditions for exercising rights before each of the AI system agents.

paragraphs: Article 11. The defense of the interests and rights provided for in this Law may be exercised:
I – before the competent administrative body;
II – in court, individually or collectively, in accordance with the provisions of the relevant legislation regarding individual, collective and diffuse protection instruments.

Article 12. Before its introduction and circulation on the market, employment or use, the AI agent may carry out a preliminary assessment to determine the level of risk of the system, based on the criteria set out in this Chapter, in accordance with the state of the art of technological development.

§ 1. Carrying out the preliminary assessment shall be considered a measure of good practice and may result in benefits for the AI agent for the purposes of the provisions of Article 50, § 1, and may even receive priority treatment in conformity assessment procedures, under the terms of Article 34, both of this Law.
§ 2. The sectoral authority shall define the circumstances in which the preliminary assessment shall be simplified or waived, in compliance with the general rules of the competent authority.

§ 3. The agent may request information from other agents of the AI systems that enables that agent to carry out a preliminary assessment, in accordance with this Law, respecting commercial and industrial secrets.

§ 4. After adversarial proceedings and the full right of defense have been ensured, the competent authority may, in collaboration with the SIA sectoral authorities and following prior notice, reclassify an AI system and order, with reasons, that an algorithmic impact assessment be conducted.
§ 5. The result of the preliminary assessment may be used by the AI agent to demonstrate compliance with the security, transparency and ethics requirements provided for in this Law.

§ 6. The sectoral authority may request the carrying out or access to the preliminary assessment of the AI system for the purposes of assessing the system's risk, respecting commercial and industrial secrets.

paragraphs: Article 13. The development, implementation and use of AI systems are prohibited:
I – for the purpose of:
a) to instigate or induce the behavior of natural persons or groups in a manner that causes harm to their own or third parties' health, safety, or other fundamental rights;

b) to exploit a vulnerability of natural persons or groups with the purpose or effect of inducing their behavior in a manner that causes harm to their own or third parties' health, safety, or other fundamental rights;
c) evaluate the personality traits, characteristics or past behavior, criminal or not, of individuals or groups, to assess the risk of committing crimes, infractions or recidivism;
d) enable the production or dissemination or facilitate the creation of material that characterizes or represents abuse or sexual exploitation of children and adolescents;

II − by public authorities, to evaluate, classify or rank natural persons, based on their social behavior or personality attributes, through universal scoring, for access to goods and services and public policies, in an illegitimate or disproportionate way;
III – in autonomous weapons systems (SAA);
IV – in remote biometric identification systems, in real time and in spaces accessible to the public, with the exception of the following circumstances:

a) initiation of an investigation or criminal proceeding, upon prior and motivated judicial authorization, when there is reasonable evidence of authorship or participation in a criminal offense, proof cannot be provided by other available means and the fact investigated does not constitute a criminal offense of lesser offensive potential;
b) search for victims of crimes and missing persons, or in circumstances that involve a serious and imminent threat to the life or physical integrity of natural persons;
c) flagrant crimes punishable by a custodial sentence of more than 2 (two) years, with immediate communication to the judicial authority;
d) recapture of escaped defendants and compliance with arrest warrants and restrictive measures ordered by the Judiciary.
§ 1. Developers of AI systems shall adopt measures to prevent the use of their systems for the circumstances described in the chapeau of this article.

§ 2. The use of systems referred to in item IV of this Article shall be proportional and strictly necessary to serve the public interest, observing due legal process and judicial control, as well as the principles and rights provided for in this Law and, where appropriate, in Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), especially the guarantee against discrimination and the need for review of algorithmic inference by the public agent responsible.

Article 14. The AI system used for the following purposes and contexts of use is considered high risk, taking into account the probability and severity of adverse impacts on affected people or groups, in accordance with regulations:

I – application as security devices in the management and operation of critical infrastructures, such as traffic control and water and electricity supply networks, when there is a relevant risk to the physical integrity of people and the interruption of essential services, illicitly or abusively, and as long as they are decisive for the result or decision, operation or access to an essential service;

II – AI systems used as a determining factor in making decisions regarding the selection of students in admission processes to educational or professional training institutions, or for determining assessments of academic progress or monitoring of students, except in cases where monitoring is carried out exclusively for security purposes;

III – recruitment, screening, filtering or evaluation of candidates, making decisions on promotions or terminations of contractual employment relationships, evaluating the performance and behavior of affected people in the areas of employment, worker management and access to self-employment;

IV – evaluation of criteria for access, eligibility, granting, review, reduction or revocation of private and public services that are considered essential, including systems used to evaluate the eligibility of natural persons for the provision of public assistance and security services;
V – evaluation and classification of calls or determination of priorities for essential public services, such as firefighters and medical assistance;

VI – administration of justice, with regard to the use of systems that assist judicial authorities in investigating facts and applying the law when there is a risk to individual freedoms and the democratic rule of law, excluding systems that assist administrative acts and activities;
VII – autonomous vehicles in public spaces, when their use may generate a relevant risk to the physical integrity of people;
VIII – applications in the health sector to assist diagnoses and medical procedures, when there is a relevant risk to people’s physical and mental integrity;

IX – analytical study of crimes relating to natural persons, allowing police authorities to search large sets of data, available in different data sources or in different formats, in order to identify behavioral patterns and profiles;

X – investigation by administrative authorities to assess the credibility of evidence in the course of investigating or prosecuting offenses, or to predict the occurrence or recurrence of an actual or potential offense on the basis of profiling natural persons;
XI – biometric identification and authentication systems for recognizing emotions, excluding biometric authentication systems whose sole purpose is to confirm a specific natural person;
XII – immigration management and border control to evaluate the entry of a person or group of people into national territory.

Sole paragraph. High-risk use is not considered to be one in which the AI system is used as an intermediary technology that does not influence or determine the result or decision or when it performs a restricted procedural task.

Article 15. The SIA shall be responsible for regulating the classification of the list of high-risk AI systems, as well as identifying new high-risk use cases, taking into account the probability and severity of adverse impacts on affected people or groups, and based on at least 1 (one) of the following criteria:
I – the system produces, illicitly or abusively, relevant legal effects and negatively impacts access to public or essential services;
II – high potential for material or moral harm, as well as illegal or abusive discriminatory bias;
III – the system significantly affects people from a vulnerable group;
IV – degree of reversibility of damage;
V – harmful history, of a relevant material or moral nature;
VI – degree of transparency, explainability and auditability of the AI system that makes its control or supervision significantly difficult;
VII – high potential for systemic harm, such as cybersecurity, and violence against vulnerable groups;

VIII – extent and probability of the risks of the AI system, including the mitigation measures adopted and considering the expected benefits, in accordance with the principles and foundations of this Law;
IX – the system poses significant risks to integral human health – physical, mental and social – in the individual and collective dimensions;
X – the system may negatively impact the development and physical, psychological or moral integrity of children and adolescents.

Article 16. Regulation of the list and classification of new high-risk AI use cases shall be preceded by a procedure that ensures public participation and a regulatory impact analysis, with the following responsibilities:
I – the competent authority, as coordinator of the SIA, guarantees the harmonious application of this Law, and shall:
a) issue general normative guidelines in relation to the impacts of AI systems on fundamental rights and freedoms or that produce relevant legal effects;
b) publish the consolidated list of all high-risk systems defined by sectoral authorities;
II – the sectoral authorities, within the scope of their attributions and on a prevalent basis, provide for the technical and specific aspects of AI applications in the regulated market, and shall:
a) establish lists of cases classified or not classified as high risk within the purposes and contexts defined in Article 14;
b) establish, precisely, the list of high-risk systems in this Law;
c) receive and analyze algorithmic impact assessments;
d) indicate, in a list, cases of use of systems or applications of AI systems that are high risk or not.

§ 1. The competent authority and sectoral authorities shall consider the state of the art of technological development and the evolution and harmonization of sectoral and non-sectoral good practices for the purposes of monitoring and continuous reclassification of high-risk AI systems.

§ 2. The developer and deployer who consider that the AI system does not fit into the high-risk classification may submit a reasoned petition to the sectoral authorities together with their preliminary assessment, in accordance with the regulations.
§ 3. Distributors shall ensure that the AI system complies with the governance measures provided for in this Law before being put into circulation on the market.

§ 4. The competent authority and sectoral authorities may accredit conformity assessment bodies that offer AI agents identification and risk classification services for the use of AI systems, in accordance with applicable national and international technical standards.
§ 5. When classifying new applications of high-risk AI systems, sectoral authorities shall:

I – expressly indicate its legal and administrative consequences and the conditions for regularization to occur in a proportional and equitable manner, with no burden or losses being imposed on the affected individuals which, depending on the peculiarities of the case, are abnormal or excessive;
II – consider the real obstacles and difficulties faced by AI agents and the requirements of public policies in their charge, without prejudice to the rights of affected people and groups;

III – provide for a transition regime so that new obligations and duties are fulfilled in a proportional, equitable and efficient manner and without prejudice to the interests of people and groups affected by AI systems.
§ 6. The procedure referred to in the chapeau of this Article shall afford the affected productive and economic sectors an opportunity to make submissions.
Article 17. AI agents shall guarantee the security of systems and comply with the rights of affected people or groups, in accordance with regulations.
units.fullText: Article 17. AI agents shall guarantee the security of systems and comply with the rights of affected people or groups, in accordance with regulations.

paragraphs: Article 18. In addition to complying with the provisions of Section I of this Chapter, the developer and deployer, when introducing or putting into circulation on the market a high-risk system, shall adopt, among others, the following governance measures and the following internal processes, in accordance with the state of the art of technological development and with reasonable efforts:
I – for the deployer:
a) documentation, in an appropriate format, covering all relevant stages of the system's life cycle;

b) use of tools or processes to record the results of the system's use, so as to permit assessment of its accuracy and robustness, detection of potentially unlawful or abusive discriminatory results, and implementation of the adopted risk-mitigation measures;
c) documentation of testing to assess appropriate levels of reliability and safety;
d) documentation in an appropriate format of the degree of human supervision that contributed to the results presented by the AI systems;
e) measures to mitigate and prevent discriminatory biases, when the risk of discrimination arises from the application of the AI system;

f) provision of adequate information that allows, respecting industrial and commercial secrecy in accordance with its technical capabilities, the interpretation of results and the operation of AI systems introduced or put into circulation on the market;
II – for the developer:

a) maintaining a record of the governance measures adopted in the development of the AI system, to provide the necessary information to the deployer so that the latter complies with the obligations determined in item I of the chapeau of this article, in accordance with the legal relationship established between the parties and subject to commercial and industrial secrecy;
b) use of tools or processes for recording the system's operation, in order to allow the assessment of its accuracy and robustness;
c) carrying out tests to assess appropriate levels of safety;

d) adoption of technical measures to enable the applicability of the results of AI systems and the provision of adequate information that allows the interpretation of their results and their operation, respecting industrial and commercial secrecy;
e) measures to mitigate and prevent discriminatory biases, when the risk of discrimination arises from the application of the AI system;
f) transparency about management and governance policies to promote social and sustainable responsibility, within the scope of its activities.

§ 1. The sectoral authorities shall define the circumstances in which the obligations established in regulation shall be relaxed or waived, according to the context in which the AI agent operates in the value chain of the AI system.

§ 2. Distributors shall support and verify that the AI system complies with the governance measures provided for in this Law, before the system is put into circulation on the market, in accordance with the regulations.

§ 3. Agents in the AI system or application value chain shall cooperate with each other, making the necessary information available and providing the technical access and assistance reasonably expected and necessary to fulfill the obligations established in this article, safeguarding industrial and commercial secrecy.

§ 4. The governance measures and internal processes provided for in this article, to be adopted by the agents, shall correspond to the respective phase of the life cycle of the AI system for which they are responsible, according to the level of knowledge about the respective project, implementation, application and use.
§ 5. If the deployer or distributor makes a substantial modification or changes the purpose of an AI system, it shall be considered a developer for the purposes of this Law.

Article 19. When the AI system generates synthetic content, it shall, considering the state of the art of technological development and the context of use, include an identifier in such content to verify authenticity or characteristics of its origin, modifications or transmission, as per regulation.
§ 1. The presence of the identifier provided for in the chapeau does not satisfy other information and transparency requirements, as well as other parameters to be defined in regulation.

§ 2. The competent authority, in collaboration with the Permanent Council for Regulatory Cooperation on Artificial Intelligence (Cria), shall make a software library available with a view to facilitating compliance with the signaling obligation, ideally adopting a widely recognized international standard.

§ 3. The use of synthetic content in works with artistic, cultural or entertainment purposes may, whenever it does not represent a risk of disseminating false information, be signaled by means that do not compromise the usefulness and quality of the work, such as in the credits or metadata associated with such work, preserving its enjoyment by the public and its conventional uses.

paragraphs: Article 20. To seek to mitigate risks related to the production and circulation of synthetic content, the public authorities, together with the private sector, civil society and research and development professionals, shall, in the form of regulation, promote capabilities to identify and label synthetic content produced by AI systems and establish the authenticity and provenance of the digital content produced.
units.fullText: Article 20. To seek to mitigate risks related to the production and circulation of synthetic content, the public authorities, together with the private sector, civil society and research and development professionals, shall, in the form of regulation, promote capabilities to identify and label synthetic content produced by AI systems and establish the authenticity and provenance of the digital content produced.

paragraphs: Article 21. High-risk AI agents shall ensure that their systems are in accordance with the governance measures defined throughout Chapter IV of this Law, as well as in other relevant legislation, especially in their respective sector.
units.fullText: Article 21. High-risk AI agents shall ensure that their systems are in accordance with the governance measures defined throughout Chapter IV of this Law, as well as in other relevant legislation, especially in their respective sector.

paragraphs: Article 22. When developing, contracting or adopting high-risk AI systems, the public authority shall guarantee:
I – access to databases and full data portability of Brazilian citizens and public management, in accordance with Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law);
II – the minimum standardization of systems in terms of their data architecture and metadata, in order to promote interoperability between systems and good data governance.

Article 23. In addition to the governance measures established in this Chapter, all direct and indirect Public Administration entities, when developing or using high-risk AI systems, shall adopt the following measures:
I – definition of system access and use protocols that allow recording of who used it, for what specific situation and for what purpose;

II – facilitated and effective guarantee to the citizen, before the public authorities, of the right to human explanation and review of decisions by AI systems that generate relevant legal effects or that significantly impact the interests of the affected person, to be promoted by the competent public agent;

III – publication in easily accessible vehicles, preferably on their websites, of preliminary assessments of high-risk AI systems developed, implemented or used by public authorities in the Union, States, Federal District and Municipalities.

§ 1. The use of biometric systems for identification purposes shall comply with the principles and governance measures provided for in this Law and shall be preceded by an algorithmic impact assessment, observing the guarantees for the exercise of the rights of affected people or groups and protection against direct, indirect, illegal or abusive discrimination.

§ 2. If it is impossible to eliminate or substantially mitigate the risks associated with the AI system identified in the algorithmic impact assessment provided for in Section IV of this Chapter, its use shall be discontinued.
§ 3. The measures provided for in this article also apply to AI systems used by companies responsible for the management or execution of public services.
§ 4. High-risk AI systems already implemented by the public authorities when this Law was published shall adapt within a reasonable period of time, to be defined by the competent authority.

§ 5. The provisions set out in the chapeau of this article cover bodies of the Legislative and Judiciary Powers, when performing administrative functions, and legal entities under private law responsible for the management or execution of public services, when involved in these activities.

paragraphs: Article 24. The federal Executive Branch shall set minimum transparency standards for AI systems used by bodies and entities in the federal public sector, in addition to regularly monitoring compliance with these obligations.

Sole paragraph. The federal Executive Branch shall promote transparency in AI systems used by public bodies and entities with the aim of promoting and consolidating responsible and open governance practices.
units.fullText:
Article 24. The federal Executive Branch shall set minimum transparency standards for AI systems used by bodies and entities in the federal public sector, in addition to regularly monitoring compliance with these obligations.

Sole paragraph. The federal Executive Branch shall promote transparency in AI systems used by public bodies and entities with the aim of promoting and consolidating responsible and open governance practices.

paragraphs: Article 25. The assessment of the algorithmic impact of AI systems is the obligation of the developer or deployer who introduces or puts an AI system into circulation on the market, whenever the system or its use is high risk, considering the role and participation of the agent in the chain.

§ 1. The developer of a high-risk AI system shall, in accordance with the regulations, share preliminary and algorithmic impact assessments with the sectoral authority, whose methodology shall consider and record, at least, an assessment of the risks and benefits to fundamental rights, mitigation measures and the effectiveness of these management measures.
§ 2. The AI agent may request from other agents in the chain, respecting industrial and commercial secrets, the information necessary to carry out the algorithmic impact assessment.
§ 3. The evaluation shall be carried out prior to and in accordance with the specific context of the introduction or placing into circulation on the market of the AI system.

§ 4. The sectoral authority shall define the circumstances in which the algorithmic impact assessment shall be made more flexible, taking into account the context of action and the role of each of the AI agents and the general rules of the competent authority.

§ 5. The competent authority, based on the Cria guidelines, shall establish general criteria and elements for the preparation of an algorithmic impact assessment and the frequency of its update, considering the life cycle of high-risk AI systems.

§ 6. The sectoral authority, based on the state of the art of technological development and best practices, shall regulate the criteria and frequency of updating algorithmic impact assessments, considering the life cycle of high-risk AI systems.

§ 7. AI agents who, after the introduction of an AI system into the market or its use in service, become aware of an unexpected and relevant risk or impact that the system presents to the rights of natural persons shall immediately communicate the fact to the sectoral authority and other agents in the chain so that all appropriate measures can be taken, including, when necessary, notifying people and groups affected by the AI system.

§ 8. The competent authority and the sectoral authorities shall establish the circumstances in which public participation shall be necessary, as well as the circumstances in which it can be carried out in a simplified manner, indicating the criteria for this participation.

paragraphs: Article 26. The algorithmic impact assessment shall be carried out prior to the introduction or release into circulation of the AI system, and shall consist of a continuous interactive process, carried out throughout the entire life cycle of high-risk AI systems, requiring periodic updates.
Sole paragraph. Considering any existing sectoral regulations, The competent authority, in collaboration with the other SIA entities, shall define:

I – general parameters regarding the frequency of updating algorithmic impact assessments, which shall be carried out at least when there are significant changes to the systems, in accordance with the regulations;
II – the circumstances in which the algorithmic impact assessment shall be simplified, considering the type of AI system agents.

paragraphs: Article 27. If the AI agent has to prepare an impact report on the protection of personal data, in accordance with Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), the algorithmic impact assessment may be carried out in conjunction with the aforementioned document.

If the AI agent has to prepare an impact report on the protection of personal data, in accordance with Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), the algorithmic impact assessment may be carried out in conjunction with the aforementioned document.

paragraphs: Article 28. The conclusions of the algorithmic impact assessment shall be public, subject to industrial and commercial secrets, in accordance with the regulations.

paragraphs: Article 29. The developer of general-purpose and generative AI systems shall carry out, in addition to the relevant documentation on the development of the system, its preliminary assessment, in order to identify its respective expected risk levels, including potential systemic risk.
Sole paragraph. The preliminary assessment shall consider the reasonably expected purposes of use and the criteria provided, in accordance with Section III of Chapter III of this Law.

Article 29. The developer of general-purpose and generative AI systems shall carry out, in addition to the relevant documentation on the development of the system, its preliminary assessment, in order to identify its respective expected risk levels, including potential systemic risk.

Sole paragraph. The preliminary assessment shall consider the reasonably expected purposes of use and the criteria provided, in accordance with Section III of Chapter III of this Law.

paragraphs: Article 30. Before making a general-purpose and generative AI system with systemic risk available or placing it on the market for commercial purposes, its developer shall ensure compliance with the following requirements:
I – describe the general-purpose AI model;
II – document the tests and analyzes carried out, in order to identify and manage reasonably predictable risks, as appropriate and technically feasible;
III – document the non-mitigable risks remaining after development;

IV – process and incorporate only sets of data collected and processed in accordance with legal requirements and subject to adequate data governance, especially when dealing with personal data, in accordance with Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law) and Chapter II of this Law;
V – publish a summary of the data set used in training the system, in accordance with regulations;

VI – design and develop general-purpose and generative AI systems using applicable standards to, considering the context of use, reduce energy use, resource use and waste, as well as to increase energy efficiency and the overall efficiency of the system;
VII – prepare technical documentation and intelligible instructions for use, in order to allow developers, distributors and deployers to have clarity about how the system works.

§ 1. Compliance with the requirements established in this article does not depend on whether the system is provided as a stand-alone model or incorporated into another AI system or product, or provided under free and open source licenses, as a service, as well as through other distribution channels.
§ 2. Developers of general-purpose and generative AI systems may formulate codes of good practice, or adhere to them, to demonstrate compliance with the obligations stipulated in this article.

Article 32. Developers of general-purpose and generative AI systems made available as a resource for the development of services by third parties, such as those provided through API or other integration models, shall cooperate, to the extent of their participation, with other AI agents throughout the period in which this service is provided and supported, in order to allow adequate mitigation of risks and compliance with the rights established in this Law.
units.fullText: Article 32. Developers of general-purpose and generative AI systems made available as a resource for the development of services by third parties, such as those provided through API or other integration models, shall cooperate, to the extent of their participation, with other AI agents throughout the period in which this service is provided and supported, in order to allow adequate mitigation of risks and compliance with the rights established in this Law.

paragraphs: Article 33. The competent authority, in collaboration with the other SIA entities, shall define in which cases the obligations provided for in this Section shall be simplified or waived, according to the risk involved and the state of the art of technological development.
Sole paragraph. The provisions of Chapter VI apply, where applicable, and the competent authority is responsible for approving codes of conduct and self-regulation of general-purpose AI systems.

Article 33. The competent authority, in collaboration with the other SIA entities, shall define in which cases the obligations provided for in this Section shall be simplified or waived, according to the risk involved and the state of the art of technological development.

paragraphs: Article 34. The competent authority and sectoral authorities may accredit national or international conformity assessment bodies specialized in the governance of AI systems to assess compliance with the governance measures and internal processes required by regulatory bodies.
§ 1. The SIA shall establish the period of validity of the accreditation and the requirements for its renewal, in accordance with applicable national and international technical standards.

§ 2. The assessment of compliance with the use of AI systems shall adopt an efficient procedure that is compatible with market dynamics, without compromising the quality and reliability of the process.

§ 3. The general criteria for accreditation of conformity assessment bodies shall be established by the competent authority, and the specific criteria, together with the sectoral authorities, in line with international technical standards and considering the specificities of AI systems.
§ 4. The competent authority shall maintain a public and updated register of accredited conformity assessment bodies, including the scope of their accreditation.

§ 5. Accredited conformity assessment bodies shall be subject to continuous monitoring and periodic reassessments to ensure the maintenance of their technical competence and compliance with accreditation requirements.
§ 6. Conformity assessment may be carried out at different levels, considering the complexity and potential risk of AI systems, as defined in specific regulations.

§ 7. The competent authority and sectoral authorities may jointly establish cooperation and mutual recognition agreements with international accreditation bodies, aiming to facilitate the recognition of conformity assessments carried out in other countries.

paragraphs: Article 35. Civil liability arising from damages caused by AI systems within the scope of consumer relations remains subject to the liability rules provided for in Law No. 8,078, of September 11, 1990 (Consumer Defense Code), and in the relevant legislation, without prejudice to the application of the other rules of this Law.
units.fullText: Article 35. Civil liability arising from damages caused by AI systems within the scope of consumer relations remains subject to the liability rules provided for in Law No. 8,078, of September 11, 1990 (Consumer Defense Code), and in the relevant legislation, without prejudice to the application of the other rules of this Law.

paragraphs: Article 36. Civil liability arising from damages caused by AI systems operated, employed or used by AI agents remains subject to the liability rules provided for in Law No. 10,406, of January 10, 2002 (Civil Code), and in special legislation, without prejudice to the application of the other rules of this Law.
Sole paragraph. The specific definition of the civil liability regime applicable to damages caused by AI systems shall take into account the following criteria, unless otherwise provided by law:
I – the level of autonomy of the AI system and its degree of risk, as regulated by this Law;
II – the nature of the agents involved and the consequent existence of a specific civil liability regime in the legislation.

paragraphs: Article 37. The judge shall reverse the burden of proof when the victim is at a procedural or evidentiary disadvantage, or when the operating characteristics of the AI system make it excessively burdensome for the victim to prove the elements of civil liability.

paragraphs: Article 38. Participants in the AI regulation testing environment remain responsible, in accordance with applicable legislation, for any damages inflicted on third parties as a result of the experimentation taking place in the testing environment.

paragraphs: Article 39. The grounds for liability provided for by specific legislation remain in force.
units.fullText: Article 39. The grounds for liability provided for by specific legislation remain in force.

paragraphs: Article 40. AI agents may, individually or through associations, formulate codes of good practice and governance that establish the organizational conditions, operating regime, procedures, including complaints from affected people, security standards, technical standards, specific obligations for each sectoral context of implementation, educational actions, internal supervision and risk mitigation mechanisms and appropriate technical and organizational security measures for managing risks arising from the application of AI systems in their respective domain of activity.

§ 1. When establishing rules of good practice, the purpose, probability and severity of the risks and benefits arising from the application of AI systems and the possible impacts on vulnerable groups shall be considered, following the example of the methodology set out in Section IV of Chapter IV.
§ 2. Developers and deployers of AI systems may implement a governance program that, in accordance with the state of the art of technological development:

I – demonstrates their commitment to adopting internal processes and policies that ensure comprehensive compliance with standards and good practices concerning non-maleficence and proportionality between the methods used and the defined, legitimate purposes of AI systems;
II – is adapted to the structure, scale, and volume of their operations, as well as their potential for harm and benefit;
III – aims to establish a relationship of trust with affected persons and groups through transparent action that ensures participation mechanisms, as provided in Section IV of Chapter IV of this Law;
IV – be integrated into its general governance structure and establish and apply internal and external supervision mechanisms;
V – have response plans to reverse the possible harmful results of the AI system;
VI – be constantly updated based on information obtained from continuous monitoring and periodic assessments;
VII – have internal mechanisms and procedures for integrity, auditing, encouraging the reporting of irregularities and effective application of codes of ethics.

§ 3. Voluntary adherence to a code of good practices and the development of governance measures may be considered an indication of good faith on the part of the agent and shall be taken into consideration by the competent authority and other sectoral authorities for the purposes of applying administrative sanctions.
§ 4. The sectoral authorities shall:
I – approve codes of good conduct regarding the sphere of competence granted by law, always informing the competent authority;
II – observe the general guidelines and standards for the procedure for analysis, publication and periodic updating of the code of conduct issued by the competent authority.

Article 41. AI agents may voluntarily associate themselves in the form of a non-profit private legal entity to promote self-regulation with the aim of encouraging and ensuring better governance practices throughout the entire life cycle of AI systems.
§ 1. Self-regulation may comprise the following functions:

I – establishment of technical criteria for applied AI systems, including standardization, prudential criteria and concerted action by associated entities, as long as they do not impede technological development and are in compliance with this Law and the binding SIA standards;
II – sharing of experiences on the use of AI, with the sharing of competitively sensitive information being prohibited, in accordance with the relevant legislation;
III – contextual definition of governance structures provided for in this Law;
IV – criteria for requesting that the competent authority and the other authorities forming part of the SIA adopt a precautionary measure;
V – creation of a channel for receiving relevant information about the risks of using AI by its associates or any interested party;
VI – adoption of internationally recognized standards, best practices and certification models.

§ 2. The association between AI agents for self-regulation purposes shall observe the precepts of Law No. 12,529, of November 30, 2011 (Competition Defense Law), prohibiting any action that could restrict free competition.

Article 42. The AI agent shall communicate, within a period to be established, to the sectoral authority the occurrence of a serious security incident, including when there is a risk to the life and physical integrity of people, the interruption of critical infrastructure operations and serious damage to property or the environment, as well as serious violations of fundamental rights, the integrity of information, freedom of expression and the democratic process, in accordance with the regulations.

§ 1. The reporting obligation shall apply once the sectoral authority has defined the deadline and the criteria for determining the severity of an incident, taking account of the characteristics of AI systems and the state of the art of technological development.
§ 2. The sectoral authority shall assess the severity of the incident and may, where necessary, order the agent to take steps and measures to reverse or mitigate its effects.

paragraphs: Article 43. AI agents, in addition to the provisions of this Law, remain subject to specific legislation relating to cybersecurity and the protection of critical infrastructures, the life and physical integrity of people, damage to property and the environment, fundamental rights and the democratic process.

AI agents, in addition to the provisions of this Law, remain subject to specific legislation relating to cybersecurity and the protection of critical infrastructures, the life and physical integrity of people, damage to property and the environment, fundamental rights and the democratic process.

paragraphs: Article 44. The competent authority, in collaboration with sectoral authorities, shall create and maintain a high-risk AI database, accessible to the public, which contains public impact assessment documents, respecting commercial and industrial secrets, in accordance with regulations and in accordance with the relevant legislation, in particular Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), and Law No. 12,527, of November 18, 2011 (Access to Information Law).

Sole paragraph. Creation of the central database does not preclude the creation of sectoral high-risk AI databases; the databases shall be maintained in an interoperable, structured-data format to facilitate shared use.

paragraphs: Article 45. The Executive Branch is authorized to establish the National System for AI Regulation and Governance (SIA).
§ 1. The SIA includes:
I – the National Data Protection Authority (ANPD), the competent authority that shall coordinate the SIA;
II – sectoral authorities;
III – the Permanent Council for Regulatory Cooperation on Artificial Intelligence (Cria), observed and limited to the provisions of Section IV of this Chapter;
IV – the Committee of Artificial Intelligence Experts and Scientists (Cecia), observed and limited to the provisions of Section V of this Chapter.
§ 2. An act of the federal Executive Branch shall define the list of bodies and entities that shall form part of the SIA, in accordance with items II, III, and IV of § 1 of this Article.
§ 3. The SIA’s objectives and foundations are:

I – value and reinforce the regulatory, sanctioning and normative competencies of the sectoral authorities in harmony with the general related competencies of the competent authority that coordinates the SIA; and
II – seek harmonization and collaboration with regulatory bodies on cross-cutting themes.
§ 4. The competent authority shall coordinate Cria, as set out in Section IV of this Chapter, in order to harmonize and facilitate regulatory, supervisory and sanctioning powers.
units.fullText:
Article 45. The Executive Branch is authorized to establish the National System for AI Regulation and Governance (SIA).

§ 1. The SIA includes:

I – the National Data Protection Authority (ANPD), the competent authority that shall coordinate the SIA;

II – sectoral authorities;

III – the Permanent Council for Regulatory Cooperation on Artificial Intelligence (Cria), observed and limited to the provisions of Section IV of this Chapter;

IV – the Committee of Artificial Intelligence Experts and Scientists (Cecia), observed and limited to the provisions of Section V of this Chapter.

§ 2. An act of the federal Executive Branch shall define the list of bodies and entities that shall form part of the SIA, in accordance with items II, III, and IV of § 1 of this Article.

§ 3. The SIA’s objectives and foundations are:

I – value and reinforce the regulatory, sanctioning and normative competencies of the sectoral authorities in harmony with the general related competencies of the competent authority that coordinates the SIA; and

II – seek harmonization and collaboration with regulatory bodies on cross-cutting themes.

§ 4. The competent authority shall coordinate Cria, as set out in Section IV of this Chapter, in order to harmonize and facilitate regulatory, supervisory and sanctioning powers.

paragraphs: Article 46. As coordinator of the SIA and without prejudice to the powers provided for in Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), the competent authority is responsible for:
I – act in the representation of Brazil before international AI organizations, under the coordination of the Executive Branch;
II – issue, in collaboration with the other members of the SIA, binding rules of a general nature on the following topics:
a) form and requirements of information to be published on the use of AI systems, respecting industrial and commercial secrets;
b) procedures and requirements for preparing the algorithmic impact assessment;
c) procedures for reporting serious incidents, particularly when they affect fundamental rights;
III – issue general rules on AI in the country, supporting sectoral bodies, which are responsible for issuing specific rules;
IV – enter into regulatory agreements with SIA members to define specific rules and procedures for coordinating competencies;

V – issue general regulatory guidelines on certificates and accreditation of certification bodies with the aim of encouraging and ensuring best governance practices throughout the entire life cycle of AI systems;
VI – encourage the adoption of internationally recognized standards, best practices and certifications;
VII – receive and process anonymous complaints, establishing mechanisms to reserve the identity of the complainant;
VIII – prepare annual reports on its activities.

Sole paragraph. In experimental regulatory environments (regulatory sandboxes) involving AI systems, conducted by sectoral authorities, the competent authority shall be informed and may express its opinion regarding compliance with the purposes and principles of this Law.

Article 47. As a residual regulator, the competent authority shall exercise full normative, regulatory, supervisory and sanctioning competence for the development, implementation and use of AI systems for economic activities in which there is no specific sectoral regulatory body or entity.
units.fullText: Article 47. As a residual regulator, the competent authority shall exercise full normative, regulatory, supervisory and sanctioning competence for the development, implementation and use of AI systems for economic activities in which there is no specific sectoral regulatory body or entity.

paragraphs: Article 48. The sectoral authority is responsible for:
I – exercise regulatory, supervisory and sanctioning powers, in accordance with its sphere of competence granted by law, for the development, implementation and use of AI systems;
II – issue specific rules for the application of AI, including aspects related to high-risk activities, observing the general rules issued by the competent authority;
III – promote and encourage the provisions of Chapter VI of this Law for AI systems that fall within their sphere of competence granted by law;
IV – regarding its sphere of competence granted by law, encourage the adoption of internationally recognized standards, best practices and certification and accreditation models;
V – supervise the governance measures appropriate to each application or use of AI systems that it classifies as high risk, in order to promote:
a) harmonization with national legislation and international standards to allow technical and jurisdictional interoperability of systems and applications developed and implemented in the country;

b) the adoption of regulatory instruments that promote responsible innovation, such as experimental regulatory environments (regulatory sandboxes), self-regulation and certifications of good practices and governance;

VI – enter into, at any time, a commitment with AI agents to eliminate irregularities, legal uncertainty or contentious situations within the scope of administrative processes, in accordance with the provisions of Decree-Law No. 4,657, of September 4, 1942 (Law of Introduction to the Norms of Brazilian Law).
Article 49. The competent authority is responsible for:
I – ensure the protection of fundamental rights and other rights affected by the use of AI systems;
II – promote and encourage the provisions of Chapter VI of this Law;
III – promote cooperation actions with authorities to protect and promote the development and use of AI systems from other countries, of an international or transnational nature;

IV – request, at any time, public authorities that develop or use AI systems, specific information on the scope and nature of the data and other details of the processing carried out, with the possibility of issuing a complementary technical opinion to ensure compliance with this Law;

V – conclude, together with the sectoral authorities, at any time, a commitment with AI agents to eliminate irregularities, legal uncertainty or contentious situations within the scope of administrative processes, in accordance with the provisions of Decree-Law No. 4,657, of September 4, 1942 (Law of Introduction to the Norms of Brazilian Law);
VI – prepare annual reports on its activities;

VII – carry out or determine audits of high-risk AI systems or those that produce relevant legal effects when necessary to assess compliance with this Law, guaranteeing the confidential treatment of information, taking into account commercial and industrial secrets;

VIII – accredit institutions, using criteria established in regulations subject to public consultation, for access to data for audit and research purposes, guaranteeing the confidentiality of information, taking into account commercial and industrial secrets;

IX – accredit research institutions, using criteria established in regulations subject to public consultation, for access to data for research purposes, observing commercial and industrial secrets, anonymization and protection of personal data, in accordance with Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law).

§ 1. Both the competent authority and any entities accredited by it to carry out audits and for research purposes shall comply with requirements for the security and confidentiality of information and the protection of personal data, in accordance with Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), in consideration of commercial and industrial secrets.

§ 2. The competent authority, together with the sectoral authorities, may carry out investigations into high-risk AI systems, in case of suspected violation of the principles, rights and duties provided for in this Law or in the applicable sectoral legislation.

§ 3. The bodies and entities that are part of the SIA shall immediately communicate to the Administrative Council for Economic Defense (Cade) any information identified in the course of inspection activities that may be of interest for the application of Law No. 12,529, of November 30, 2011 (Competition Defense Law).

§ 4. In carrying out its duties and always within the limits of what is pertinent and necessary for the investigation of concrete signs of infringements of the economic order, Cade may order developers to grant access to the training, validation and test data sets used for the development of high-risk AI systems.
Article 50. AI agents, due to infractions committed against the rules set out in this Law, are subject to the following applicable administrative sanctions:
I – warning;

II – a simple fine, capped in the aggregate at R$ 50,000,000.00 (fifty million reais) per violation and, for a private-law legal entity, at 2% (two percent) of the gross revenue of that entity or of its group or conglomerate in Brazil in the preceding financial year, excluding taxes;
III – publicizing the infraction after its occurrence has been duly investigated and confirmed;
IV – prohibition or restriction to participate in the regulatory sandbox regime provided for in this Law, for up to 5 (five) years;
V – partial or total, temporary or definitive suspension of the development, supply or operation of the AI system;
VI – prohibition of processing certain databases.

§ 1. Sanctions shall be imposed following an administrative proceeding that affords the full right of defense, gradually and separately or cumulatively, according to the circumstances of the case and considering the following parameters and criteria:
I – the seriousness and nature of the infractions and possible violation of rights;
II – the good faith of the offender;
III – the advantage obtained or intended by the offender;
IV – the economic condition of the offender;
V – recidivism;
VI – the degree of damage;
VII – the cooperation of the offender;

VIII – the repeated and demonstrated adoption of internal mechanisms and procedures capable of minimizing risks, including algorithmic impact analysis and the effective implementation of a code of ethics;
IX – the adoption of a good practice and governance policy;
X – the prompt adoption of corrective measures;
XI – the proportionality between the severity of the offense and the intensity of the sanction;
XII – cumulation with other administrative sanctions that may have already been definitively applied for the same illicit act.

§ 2. Before or during the administrative proceeding referred to in § 1 of this Article, the competent authority may adopt preventive measures, including a coercive fine, subject to the total limit referred to in item II of the chapeau, where there is evidence or a well-founded concern that the AI agent:
I – causes or may cause irreparable injury or injury that is difficult to repair; or
II – renders the final outcome of the proceeding ineffective.

§ 3. The provisions of this article do not replace the application of administrative, civil or criminal sanctions defined in Law No. 8,078, of September 11, 1990 (Consumer Defense Code), in Law No. 13,709, of August 14, 2018 (General Personal Data Protection Law), and in specific legislation.

§ 4. In the case of the development, supply or use of excessive risk AI systems, there shall be, at a minimum, a fine and, in the case of a legal entity, partial or total, provisional or definitive suspension of its activities.
§ 5. The application of the sanctions provided for in this article does not exclude, under any circumstances, the obligation to fully repair the damage.

§ 6. The competent authority shall define, by means of its own regulation, the investigation procedure and the criteria for applying administrative sanctions to infractions of this Law, which shall be subject to:

I – public consultation and regulatory impact analysis, without prejudice to the provisions of Decree-Law No. 4,657, of September 4, 1942 (Law of Introduction to the Norms of Brazilian Law), Law No. 9,784, of January 29, 1999 (General Law of Administrative Procedure), and other pertinent legal provisions;

II – publication of methodologies objectively setting out the forms and calculation of sanctions, with detailed reasons for all their elements and a demonstration of compliance with the criteria provided in this Law.

§ 7. Items I, III, IV, V, and VI of the chapeau of this Article may be applied to public entities and bodies, without prejudice to Law No. 8,112 of 11 December 1990 (Federal Public Servants Statute), Law No. 8,429 of 2 June 1992 (Administrative Improbity Law), and Law No. 12,527 of 18 November 2011 (Access to Information Law).

Article 51. The competent authority and sectoral authorities may carry out joint investigations into high-risk AI systems in cases of suspected violation of the principles, rights and duties provided for in this Law or in applicable sectoral legislation.

paragraphs: Article 52. The bodies and entities that are part of the SIA shall immediately communicate to the Administrative Council for Economic Defense (Cade) any information identified in the course of inspection activities that may be of potential interest for the application of Law No. 12,529, of November 30, 2011 (Competition Defense Law).

Sole paragraph. In carrying out its duties and always within the limits of what is pertinent and necessary to investigate concrete signs of violations of the economic order, Cade may order developers to grant access to the training, validation and testing data sets used for the development of high-risk AI systems.

paragraphs: Article 53. The Permanent Council for Regulatory Cooperation on Artificial Intelligence (Cria) is created, which shall be responsible for producing guidelines and shall be a permanent forum for collaboration, including through technical cooperation agreements, with sectoral authorities and civil society, in order to harmonize and facilitate the exercise of the powers of the competent authority.
Sole paragraph. Cria shall:
I – suggest actions to be carried out by the SIA;
II – prepare studies and hold public debates on AI;
III – disseminate knowledge about AI.

Article 54. The Committee of Artificial Intelligence Experts and Scientists (Cecia) is created with the aim of guiding and technically and scientifically supervising the development and application of AI in a responsible manner, under the terms defined by regulation.
units.fullText: Article 54. The Committee of Artificial Intelligence Experts and Scientists (Cecia) is created with the aim of guiding and technically and scientifically supervising the development and application of AI in a responsible manner, under the terms defined by regulation.

paragraphs: Article 55. The competent authority and the sectoral authorities that make up the SIA shall promote and authorize the operation of an experimental regulatory environment for innovation in artificial intelligence (AI regulatory sandbox) on its own or for entities that request it and meet the requirements specified by this Law and in regulation, including in a public-private cooperation regime.

§ 1. The regulatory sandbox aims to facilitate the development, testing and validation of innovative AI systems for a limited period before they are placed on the market or put into service according to a specific plan, in order to develop innovative businesses in a safe manner.

§ 2. The competent authority and sectoral authorities may, individually or in collaboration, within the scope of experimental regulatory environment programs (regulatory sandbox), exclude the incidence of standards under their competence in relation to the regulated entity or groups of regulated entities.

§ 2. paragraphs: Article 56. The competent authority and the sectoral authorities forming part of the SIA shall regulate procedures for requesting and authorizing the operation of regulatory sandboxes; they may limit or terminate their operation and issue recommendations, taking into account, among other factors, the preservation of fundamental rights and the rights of potentially affected consumers, safety, and protection.

§ 1. Sectoral authorities shall provide micro and small companies, startups and public and private Scientific, Technological and Innovation Institutions (ICTs) with priority access to testing environments, to the extent that they meet the eligibility conditions, selection criteria and other regulations.
§ 2. The competent authority and sectoral authorities may establish mechanisms to reduce the regulatory costs of entities qualifying under § 1 of this Article.

paragraphs: Article 57. Participants in the AI regulation testing environment continue to be responsible, in accordance with applicable legislation, for any damages inflicted on third parties as a result of the experimentation that takes place in the testing environment.
